lessons.title: The Cell — Basic Unit of Life

content.body: All living things are made of cells. The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Some organisms like bacteria consist of only one cell (unicellular), while others like humans have trillions of cells (multicellular).

Cell Theory:
1. All living things are made of one or more cells
2. The cell is the basic unit of life
3. All cells come from pre-existing cells

There are two main types of cells: Prokaryotic cells — found in bacteria, they have no membrane-bound nucleus. Eukaryotic cells — found in plants, animals, and fungi, they have a true nucleus enclosed in a membrane.

content.title: Animal Cell Parts
content.body:
Cell membrane — controls what enters and leaves the cell
Nucleus — contains DNA and controls cell activities
Cytoplasm — jelly-like fluid where chemical reactions happen
Mitochondria — produces energy (ATP) through respiration
Ribosomes — where proteins are made
Endoplasmic reticulum — transports materials within the cell

content.title: Plant Cell — Extra Parts
content.body:
Cell wall — rigid outer layer made of cellulose, gives shape
Chloroplasts — contains chlorophyll for photosynthesis
Vacuole — large central space filled with cell sap
Plasma membrane — inside the cell wall
Plant cells differ from animal cells in three main ways: plant cells have a cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large permanent vacuole. Animal cells have none of these three features.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle controls cell activities?
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA and controls the activities of the cell.

questions.question: Where does energy production (respiration) occur?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the powerhouse of the cell — they produce energy through respiration.

questions.question: Which structure is found in plant cells but NOT animal cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a cell wall made of cellulose. Animal cells do not.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis in plant cells.

questions.question: What does the cell membrane do?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable — it controls what substances pass in and out.

questions.question: Which cells have no membrane-bound nucleus?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells (like bacteria) have no nucleus surrounded by a membrane.

questions.question: Where are proteins made inside a cell?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis in the cell.

questions.question: What fills the large vacuole in plant cells?
questions.explanation: The large central vacuole in plant cells is filled with cell sap (water and dissolved substances).

questions.question: Which statement is part of the Cell Theory?
options: All cells come from non-living matter
All living things are made of cells
questions.answer: All living things are made of cells
questions.explanation: Cell Theory states: all living things are made of cells, cells are the basic unit of life, and all cells come from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Organelles are specialised structures inside a cell, each performing a specific function. Think of them like organs inside your body — each one has a unique job that keeps the whole cell alive and working.

content.title: The Nucleus
content.body: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell. It is surrounded by a double membrane called the nuclear envelope, which has tiny holes called nuclear pores. Inside is the nucleolus and chromatin (DNA). The nucleus stores genetic information and directs all cell activities.

content.title: Mitochondria
content.body: Mitochondria have a double membrane — the outer membrane is smooth and the inner membrane is folded into structures called cristae. These folds increase surface area for energy production. Mitochondria produce ATP (adenosine triphosphate) through cellular respiration. Cells that need lots of energy (like muscle cells) have many mitochondria.

content.title: Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER)
content.body:
Rough ER — has ribosomes attached to its surface. It makes and transports proteins.
Smooth ER — has no ribosomes. It makes lipids and detoxifies harmful substances.
Both types are connected to the nuclear envelope.

content.title: Golgi Apparatus
content.body: The Golgi apparatus looks like a stack of flattened membrane sacs. It receives proteins from the rough ER, modifies them (like adding sugar molecules), packages them, and sends them to their destination — either inside the cell or out of the cell via vesicles.
Memory tip: Golgi = Post Office of the cell (receives, packages, and sends materials)

content.title: Lysosomes & Vacuoles
content.body:
Lysosomes — small sacs containing digestive enzymes. They break down old organelles, bacteria, and food particles. Found mainly in animal cells.
Vacuoles — storage sacs. Plant cells have one large central vacuole filled with cell sap that helps maintain cell shape (turgor pressure). Animal cells have small, temporary vacuoles.

questions.question: What is the function of the nuclear pores?
options: Allow materials to pass in and out of the nucleus
questions.answer: Allow materials to pass in and out of the nucleus
questions.explanation: Nuclear pores are small holes in the nuclear envelope that allow molecules like RNA to pass out of the nucleus.

questions.question: What are the folds of the inner mitochondrial membrane called?
questions.explanation: The inner mitochondrial membrane is folded into structures called cristae, which increase surface area for ATP production.

questions.question: Which type of ER has ribosomes attached to it?
questions.explanation: Rough ER has ribosomes on its surface, giving it a rough appearance. It is involved in protein synthesis and transport.

questions.question: The Golgi apparatus is often compared to which everyday object?
questions.explanation: Like a post office, the Golgi apparatus receives, modifies, packages, and sends materials to their correct destinations.

questions.question: Which organelle digests old and damaged cell parts?
questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes that break down waste materials, old organelles, and foreign particles.

questions.question: What causes turgor pressure in plant cells?
options: Large central vacuole filled with water
questions.answer: Large central vacuole filled with water
questions.explanation: The large central vacuole fills with water, pressing the cytoplasm against the cell wall, creating turgor pressure that keeps the plant firm.

questions.question: Which organelle makes lipids and detoxifies chemicals?
questions.explanation: Smooth ER (without ribosomes) is responsible for lipid synthesis and detoxification of drugs and poisons.

questions.question: A muscle cell would be expected to have many of which organelle?
questions.explanation: Muscle cells require large amounts of energy (ATP) for contraction, so they have many mitochondria.

questions.question: What does the Golgi apparatus receive from the rough ER?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus receives proteins from the rough ER, modifies them, and packages them for transport.

questions.question: Which is NOT a function of the nucleus?
questions.explanation: ATP production occurs in the mitochondria. The nucleus stores DNA, controls cell activities, and contains the nucleolus.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Cell division is the process by which one cell divides to produce two or more daughter cells. Mitosis is a type of cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells. It is used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction.

Mitosis: 1 parent cell (2n) → 2 identical daughter cells (2n)
Each daughter cell has the same number of chromosomes as the parent

content.title: Stages of Mitosis — PMAT
content.body:
P — Prophase: Chromosomes condense and become visible. The nuclear envelope breaks down. Spindle fibres begin to form.
M — Metaphase: Chromosomes line up at the centre (equator) of the cell. Spindle fibres attach to each chromosome.
A — Anaphase: Sister chromatids are pulled apart to opposite poles of the cell.
T — Telophase: Nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes. The cell begins to divide (cytokinesis).

Before mitosis begins, the cell goes through Interphase — the longest stage of the cell cycle. During interphase, the cell grows, produces proteins, and copies (replicates) its DNA so there is enough for both daughter cells.

content.title: Why Mitosis is Important
content.body:
Growth — when you grow taller, mitosis produces new cells
Repair — when you cut your skin, mitosis replaces the damaged cells
Asexual reproduction — in organisms like amoeba that reproduce by splitting

Memory trick for stages: PMAT
Promise — Me — A — Trophy
(Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase)

questions.question: How many daughter cells does mitosis produce?
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces 2 genetically identical daughter cells from one parent cell.

questions.question: During which stage do chromosomes line up at the centre of the cell?
questions.explanation: During Metaphase, chromosomes align at the equator (middle) of the cell. Spindle fibres attach to them.

questions.question: What happens during Anaphase?
questions.explanation: During Anaphase, the spindle fibres shorten and pull sister chromatids to opposite poles of the cell.

questions.question: What is the longest stage of the cell cycle called?
questions.explanation: Interphase is the longest stage. The cell grows and replicates its DNA in preparation for division.

questions.question: What is the purpose of mitosis in wound healing?
options: To repair and replace damaged cells
questions.answer: To repair and replace damaged cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis replaces damaged or dead cells with identical new cells, which is essential for wound healing.

questions.question: During Prophase, what happens to the nuclear envelope?
questions.explanation: During Prophase, the nuclear envelope (membrane around the nucleus) breaks down so chromosomes can be accessed by spindle fibres.

questions.question: Mitosis produces daughter cells that are genetically...
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the same chromosome number as the parent cell.

questions.question: Which organism uses mitosis for asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Amoeba reproduces asexually by binary fission, which is essentially mitotic division.

questions.question: What is the correct order of mitosis stages?
questions.explanation: The correct order is Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase — remembered as PMAT.

questions.question: What must happen to DNA before mitosis begins?
options: DNA must be converted to RNA
questions.explanation: During Interphase (before mitosis), DNA replication occurs so each daughter cell receives a complete copy of genetic information.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Although all cells in your body contain the same DNA, they don't all look the same or do the same job. This is because cells become specialised — they develop specific structures that allow them to perform particular functions. This process is called cell differentiation.

content.title: Red Blood Cells
content.body:
Shape: Biconcave disc (like a flattened donut)
No nucleus — more space for haemoglobin
Contains haemoglobin — carries oxygen
Small and flexible — squeezes through tiny capillaries
Function: Transport oxygen from lungs to body tissues

content.title: Nerve Cells (Neurons)
content.body:
Very long — can be over 1 metre
Have dendrites (branches) at one end to receive signals
Have an axon to carry electrical impulses
Surrounded by myelin sheath — speeds up impulse transmission
Function: Carry electrical signals around the body

content.title: Muscle Cells
content.body:
Long and fibrous shape
Contain many mitochondria for energy
Contain contractile proteins (actin and myosin)
Can shorten (contract) and relax
Function: Produce movement by contracting

content.title: Root Hair Cells (Plants)
content.body:
Have a long thin extension (root hair) to increase surface area
No chloroplasts — in the dark underground
Large vacuole
Function: Absorb water and mineral salts from soil

Key principle: Structure is always related to function.
If a cell has lots of mitochondria → needs lots of energy
If a cell has a large surface area → involved in absorption or exchange

questions.question: Why do red blood cells have no nucleus?
options: To create more space for haemoglobin
questions.answer: To create more space for haemoglobin
questions.explanation: Red blood cells lose their nucleus during development to make more room for haemoglobin, which carries oxygen.

questions.question: What is the function of haemoglobin in red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin is a red protein in red blood cells that binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What structure speeds up electrical impulse transmission in nerve cells?
questions.explanation: The myelin sheath is a fatty covering around the axon that acts as insulation and speeds up the transmission of nerve impulses.

questions.question: Why do muscle cells contain many mitochondria?
options: To produce large amounts of energy for contraction
questions.answer: To produce large amounts of energy for contraction
questions.explanation: Muscle cells need lots of ATP energy to contract repeatedly, so they have many mitochondria.

questions.question: What adaptation do root hair cells have to absorb more water?
options: Long thin extension to increase surface area
questions.answer: Long thin extension to increase surface area
questions.explanation: The root hair extension greatly increases the surface area in contact with soil water, improving absorption.

questions.question: Why do root hair cells have no chloroplasts?
options: They are underground with no light for photosynthesis
questions.answer: They are underground with no light for photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are only useful where there is light. Root hair cells are underground where there is no light, so chloroplasts would be useless.

questions.question: What shape are red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells have a biconcave disc shape — flattened with a depression on both sides. This increases surface area for gas exchange.

questions.question: What process produces specialised cells from unspecialised ones?
questions.explanation: Cell differentiation is the process by which cells develop specific structures and functions from unspecialised stem cells.

questions.question: Which cell can be over 1 metre long?
questions.explanation: Nerve cells (neurons) can be extremely long — some extend from your spine to your foot, over 1 metre.

questions.question: A cell with many mitochondria is most likely involved in...
questions.explanation: Cells that need lots of energy have many mitochondria. This includes muscle cells, kidney cells, and liver cells.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

content.body:
Light microscope: uses light and glass lenses
Max magnification ~×1500, resolution ~0.2μm
Electron microscope: uses electrons
TEM: up to ×500,000, resolution ~0.2nm
SEM: 3D surface images

Magnification = image size / actual size
Actual size = image size / magnification

Units:
1mm = 1000μm = 1,000,000nm
1μm = 1000nm

content.title: Calculating Size
content.body:
Cell image is 30mm long, magnification ×3000
Actual size = 30mm/3000 = 0.01mm = 10μm

Magnification calculation:
Image 45mm, actual 0.015mm
Mag = 45/0.015 = ×3000

Scale bar: 10μm bar = 20mm on image
Magnification = 20/0.01 = ×2000

content.title: Preparing Slides
content.body:
Wet mount: specimen in drop of water, cover slip
Staining: iodine (starch/cell wall brown), methylene blue (nuclei blue), eosin (cytoplasm pink)
Serial sections: thin slices for 3D reconstruction

questions.question: Magnification formula
questions.explanation: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size

questions.question: Cell image is 50mm, magnification ×5000. Actual size =
questions.explanation: actual=image/mag=50/5000=0.01mm=10μm

questions.question: Advantage of electron microscope over light microscope
questions.explanation: EM resolution ~1000× better than light microscope — can see ribosomes, membranes

questions.question: 1μm equals
Iodine solution stains in microscopy
questions.explanation: Iodine: stains starch blue-black, used to show plant cell walls and starch storage

questions.question: TEM (Transmission Electron Microscope) differs from SEM by
options: shooting electrons through specimen showing internal ultrastructure
questions.answer: shooting electrons through specimen showing internal ultrastructure
questions.explanation: TEM: electrons pass through ultra-thin section revealing internal organelle structure in 2D

questions.question: Scale bar on micrograph shows 10μm and measures 30mm. Magnification =
questions.explanation: mag=image bar/actual bar=30mm/0.01mm=3000

questions.question: Resolution means
options: ability to distinguish two points as separate (minimum resolvable distance)
questions.answer: ability to distinguish two points as separate (minimum resolvable distance)
questions.explanation: Resolution: smaller = better. LM ~200nm; TEM ~0.2nm

questions.question: Methylene blue stains
questions.explanation: Methylene blue: basic dye binding to negatively charged DNA/RNA — stains nuclei blue

questions.question: Artefact in microscopy means
options: structure that appears due to preparation method, NOT present in living cell
questions.answer: structure that appears due to preparation method, NOT present in living cell
questions.explanation: Artefacts: fixation, dehydration, staining can create structures not in living cells — important to recognise

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Cell Structure & Function. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Cell Structure & Function concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Cell Structure & Function builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Cell Structure & Function. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Cell Structure & Function. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Cell Structure & Function include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Cell Structure & Function. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
